title: GDPR DPIA Remediation Monitoring and Mitigation Closure
exclusions: Remediation items with formally approved timeline extensions with documented justification; items closed via alternative compensating controls accepted by the risk owner and DPO.
evidence: DPIA remediation tracking report showing open items by priority, overdue items with escalation history, closure rates, and dependency tracking for blocked items.

compliance.assurance_rationale: GDPR Art.35(7)(d) — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that GDPR Art.35(7)(d) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.1.35: GDPR DPIA Remediation Monitoring and Mitigation Closure.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.1.35' running on gdpr_dpia_mitigation_register.csv, index=itsm sourcetype="snow:sc_task", archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Create a test DPIA remediation action item with a deadline 30 days past due and status open; verify the monitoring search identifies the overdue item and escalates to the risk owner and DPO.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Create a test DPIA remediation item completed 10 days before its deadline with closure evidence attached; verify no overdue alert fires and the item shows as resolved.

dataSources: DPIA remediation trackers, project management system exports (Jira, Azure DevOps), risk register updates, and mitigation closure evidence including compensating control documentation
app: Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928), Splunk Add-on for GitHub (Splunkbase 5596)
spl:
| inputlookup gdpr_dpia_mitigation_register.csv
| rename mitigation_task as task_number
| join task_number [
    search index=itsm sourcetype="snow:sc_task" earliest=-180d
    | eval done=if(state IN ("Closed","Resolved"),1,0)
    | stats latest(state) as state latest(_time) as last_update max(done) as closed by number
    | rename number as task_number
]
| eval age_days=round((now()-strptime(due_date,"%Y-%m-%d"))/86400,0)
| where closed=0 AND age_days > 0
| table processing_activity, mitigation_description, task_number, state, due_date, age_days
| sort - age_days
description: The DPIA must include measures to address risks. This use case ties DPIA mitigation items to ITSM work orders, tracks age and overdue status, and verifies technical completion signals (e.g., control deployment logs) where available.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for GDPR compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes itsm and sourcetypes snow:sc_task. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Export mitigation action list from each approved DPIA into `gdpr_dpia_mitigation_register.csv`; (2) create linked ServiceNow tasks with due dates; (3) alert owners weekly on overdue items; (4) optionally confirm technical controls via GitHub merge events or Terraform apply logs; (5) close DPIA only when all mitigations are verified. Indexes required: `index=itsm`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=snow:sc_task`.
visualization: Table (overdue mitigations), Single value (open high-risk mitigations), Burndown chart (open vs closed), Bar chart (age by owner).
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Remediation timeline extensions** — Formally approved timeline extensions for complex mitigations (e.g., system replacements) may show items as overdue against original dates while being on track per the revised approved schedule.

2. **Dependency on third-party deliverables** — Remediation items dependent on vendor software releases or service changes may be blocked pending external delivery; document the dependency.

3. **Partial closure criteria** — Remediation items with multiple sub-tasks may show as open when some components are complete; check completion criteria for staged closure approval.

4. **Risk acceptance as alternative closure** — Items where the residual risk is formally accepted as an alternative to technical remediation should be closed as 'accepted' rather than remaining open.

5. **Compensating control implementation** — Alternative controls implemented in lieu of the originally specified remediation may not match the closure criteria literally while providing equivalent protection.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Technology Add-on**: Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase app ID 1928) must be installed on search heads and indexers. Verify with `| rest /services/apps/local | search label="Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow" | table label version`.
• **Indexes**: `itsm` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Lookups**: `gdpr_dpia_mitigation_register.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('gdpr_dpia_mitigation_register.csv')`.
• **Search-head capacity**: Subsearch-based detections need `subsearch_maxtime` ≥ 60s and `subsearch_maxout` ≥ 50000 in `limits.conf`. For high-volume environments, tune `[search] max_searches_per_cpu` up from default.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.35(7)(d)`. The control objective is: "Evidence that GDPR Art.35(7)(d) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.1.35: GDPR DPIA Remediation Monitoring and Mitigation Closure." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is performed by the Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase app ID 1928), landing events in `itsm` under `snow:sc_task`. REST-polled ticket tables produce 500-5000 records per sync per table; at 300s interval expect ~3-8 GB/month for a mid-size service desk.

Configure the input through Settings > Data Inputs > ServiceNow Service Account. A reference `inputs.conf` stanza looks like:

```ini
[snow://{instance_slug}]
url = https://<instance>.service-now.com
table = {table_name}
interval = 300
index = itsm
sourcetype = {sourcetype}
```

The following fields are authoritative for this detection once the TA parses them: `number, state, priority, assignment_group, opened_at, closed_at, sys_updated_on, caller_id, category, subcategory`. Verify they are extracted at search time with `index=itsm earliest=-15m | fieldsummary` before scheduling the detection.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Export mitigation action list from each approved DPIA into `gdpr_dpia_mitigation_register.csv`. Report-generation cadence must match the regulatory reporting cycle; a monthly digest satisfies an annual review requirement but not a quarterly one.

• **Hint 2:** create linked ServiceNow tasks with due dates. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** alert owners weekly on overdue items. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** optionally confirm technical controls via GitHub merge events or Terraform apply logs. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 5:** close DPIA only when all mitigations are verified. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "GDPR DPIA Remediation Monitoring and Mitigation Closure," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the GDPR control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time.

This search correlates the outer dataset (index=`itsm`, sourcetype=`snow:sc_task`) with a subsearch on `task_number` across the last 180 days. `type=inner` means the search only returns rows where `task_number` exists in both datasets. This is the correct choice when the detection requires presence in both sources — a left-join would include orphaned rows that cannot be evaluated against the compliance rule.

Join caveat: Splunk's `| join` subsearch is capped at `max_searches_per_cpu * subsearch_maxout` results (default: 50000). If the inner subsearch exceeds this, rows are silently dropped and the resulting report will under-count. Check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd log_level=WARN subsearch` for truncation warnings. For inner searches expected to return >10000 rows, rewrite as `| stats` with a larger base search and post-filter, or use `| tstats` if a CIM datamodel covers the data.

Time-zone sensitivity: the `strptime(due_date, "%Y-%m-%d")` conversion assumes the source timestamp is in the Splunk server's local time zone unless the format includes `%z`. If `due_date` is emitted in UTC by the source system but Splunk's search head runs in a non-UTC zone, the resulting epoch will be off by the zone offset and the subsequent `where _time > due_date_epoch` comparison will produce systematic false positives or negatives. Always validate by comparing `strftime(due_date_epoch, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M %Z")` against a known-good reference event before promoting to production.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.1.35: GDPR DPIA Remediation Monitoring and Mitigation Closure]
cron_schedule = 0 */6 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -180d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.1.35,reg=GDPR
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=itsm sourcetype=snow:sc_task earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `age_days`, `closed`, `done`, `due_date`, `last_update`, `mitigation_description`, `processing_activity`, `state`, `task_number`. Run:

```spl
index=itsm sourcetype=snow:sc_task earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table age_days, closed, done, due_date, last_update, mitigation_description, processing_activity, state
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `age_days`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(age_days) as p50 perc90(age_days) as p90 perc99(age_days) as p99 max(age_days) as hi
```

The current threshold `0` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.1.35: GDPR evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Drill-through table (`overdue mitigations`):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 2 — Single-value KPI (`open high-risk mitigations`):** Place at top-left. Configure `underLabel` with the regulation clause (e.g. 'GDPR current violation count') and set a red colour range for any non-zero value. A zero reading is the steady state for a healthy control.

**Panel 3 — Burndown Chart (`open vs closed`):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 4 — Distribution (`age by owner`):** Shows the volumetric distribution across the primary dimension. Use `sort` so the worst offender is first — auditors focus on the tallest bar.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** The most likely cause is subsearch truncation (see Step 2). Inspect `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=searchprocessor log_level=WARN subsearch` for messages like 'Output result count exceeded 50000.'

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**GDPR-specific failure modes:**

• Lawful basis drift: a detection that worked when the processing activity had consent as its lawful basis becomes misleading when the business team silently changes the RoPA (Record of Processing Activities) entry to 'legitimate interest.' Every GDPR compliance search should reference the RoPA entry ID in its comments and re-review the detection when the RoPA is revised — at minimum annually.

• Data Subject Rights timers: Article 12(3) requires response to access/erasure/portability requests within one month. Detections that measure DSR fulfilment must use business-day clocks (not wall-clock 30 days) for regulators that enforce the distinction; the threshold value in the search should align with the local Supervisory Authority's interpretation, not the ISO 8601 literal.

• Cross-border transfer fragility: Schrems II invalidated the EU-US Privacy Shield in 2020 and the EU-US Data Privacy Framework (2023) replaced it; any detection involving transfer monitoring must key off the current adequacy decision list, not a hardcoded country allowlist. Maintain `gdpr_adequacy_list.csv` and refresh when the EDPB updates the adequacy register.

• Breach notification 72-hour window (Art. 33): the clock starts at awareness, not at the forensically-confirmed moment of compromise. Detections must capture the first-awareness event (SOC alert acknowledgment, first ticket open, first email notification) — not just the incident-resolved timestamp — or the notification SLA metric will understate breaches that took 72h+1h to confirm.
grandmaExplanation: We the DPIA must include measures to address risks so we can show we meet our privacy duties when people ask for proof.
